aliases: 印度菠菜芝士咖喱 (Yìndù bōcài zhīshì gālí)
North Indian spinach and paneer curry

english: North Indian spinach and paneer curry
chinese: 印度菠菜芝士咖喱 (Yìndù bōcài zhīshì gālí)

food_subgroup: North Indian spinach curry with paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for palak paneer as a specific dish. Closest parent concept: spinach-based curry with dairy cheese (paneer). FoodOn does not yet have a dedicated node for this dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: North Indian spinach curry with paneer cottage cheese
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0SAJ used as closest parent for mixed spinach-dairy curry dish. No exact FoodEx2 node for palak paneer.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for palak paneer as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of palak paneer is approximately 250g, comprising roughly 80g of paneer cubes in a creamy spinach-onion-tomato-spice gravy, as served at North Indian restaurants such as Saravana Bhavan in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Palak Paneer — ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research - National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, adapted for a standard North Indian restaurant preparation of palak paneer (approximately 32% paneer by weight in spinach-based gravy with cream/butter). Values represent a composite dish profile consistent with ICMR-NIN spinach and paneer component data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Indian Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database and Peer-Reviewed Literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed GI literature for Indian dairy-spinach dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for palak paneer in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~25 (low) based on analogous Indian mixed dishes dominated by paneer (high-protein dairy, PDCAAS 1.0), cream/butter (high fat), and spinach (high fibre, very low digestible carbohydrate). The very low digestible carbohydrate content (~4.8g/100g) and high protein/fat matrix strongly attenuate glycemic response. GL per 250g serving = (25 × 12g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.0, rounded to 2.5 consistent with input source. Estimation basis: University of Sydney GI Database analogous dairy-vegetable curry entries and Augustin et al. (2015) peer-reviewed GI methodology.

hypertension.guidance: Palak paneer provides approximately 500mg sodium per standard 250g restaurant serving, which represents about 22% of the 2300mg daily sodium limit; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~25) and glycemic load (~2.5) per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Palak paneer is one of the most suitable North Indian restaurant dishes for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (~25) and GL (~2.5 per serving), driven by high paneer protein (casein, PDCAAS 1.0), dairy fat, and spinach fibre; it is best paired with roti or paratha rather than rice to keep the overall meal glycemic load low.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~11.25g per 250g serving from paneer and cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 250g serving of palak paneer contributes approximately 11.25g of saturated fat (primarily from paneer and cream/butter), which is a significant proportion of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency and request reduced cream preparation where possible, while benefiting from the dish's high potassium (500mg/serving) and spinach-derived folate (100mcg/serving) which support vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium from paneer supports bone health; spinach iron is poorly bioavailable due to oxalate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Palak paneer provides approximately 425mg calcium per serving (from paneer and cream), which supports bone health relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, and delivers beneficial folate (~100mcg) and Vitamin A (~300mcg RAE); however, the spinach iron (~5mg/serving) is poorly bioavailable due to oxalate content (~300mg/100g cooked spinach) and should not be relied upon as a primary iron source.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Palak Paneer is a classic North Indian (Punjabi) vegetarian curry consisting of soft cubes of fresh Indian cottage cheese (paneer) cooked in a silky, vibrant green gravy made from blanched and pureed spinach (palak). The dish is prepared by first blanching spinach to preserve its colour and nutrients, then pureeing it into a smooth base. A tempering of cumin seeds, garlic, ginger, and green chilies is prepared in oil or ghee, followed by onion and tomato, and the spinach puree is added with mild spices including turmeric and garam masala. Paneer cubes are folded in and the dish is finished with fresh cream or butter for richness. The texture is creamy and smooth with soft paneer pieces; the flavour is earthy, mildly spiced, and slightly rich. Per 250g restaurant serving: approximately 300 kcal, 18g protein (paneer casein, PDCAAS 1.0), 20g fat (11.25g saturated from paneer and cream), 12g carbohydrate, 500mg sodium. GI ~25 (low), GL ~2.5 (very low). CLINICAL NOTE: Spinach iron (~5mg/serving) is poorly bioavailable due to oxalate content — do not recommend as a primary iron source. Calcium is highly bioavailable from paneer (~425mg/serving). Vitamin A ~300mcg RAE per serving. Vitamin K is very high (~950mcg/serving) due to spinach. Source: ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017. Widely served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore including Saravana Bhavan and Ananda Bhavan, and at Indian thali meals and wedding banquets.
common_user_queries: is palak paneer high in fat

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are well-supported. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, clearly documented. Confidence is high for macros and most micronutrients; copper, manganese, and vitamin K have slightly lower individual confidence (0.9) but do not affect overall record confidence.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero — palak paneer contains no industrially produced trans fats; any trace ruminant trans fat from dairy is below detection threshold and reported as 0.0 per ICMR-NIN source). Spinach iron is non-haem and poorly bioavailable due to oxalate (~300mg/100g cooked spinach); do not recommend palak paneer as a primary iron source. Vitamin K is very high (~380mcg/100g; ~950mcg per 250g serving) due to spinach content — individuals on warfarin/anticoagulant therapy should maintain consistent intake and consult their clinician. Copper and manganese values sourced from enriched_json with confidence 0.9. Vitamin K value sourced from enriched_json with confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for palak paneer exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. FoodEx2 code A0SAJ used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 node for palak paneer. FoodOn URI not available for this dish.
